refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output (energy_per_ton) to support evaluation against a customer-defined energy efficiency target.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the capability to measure/report energy efficiency metrics, and (2) the achievement of a performance target. The refined FR focuses on the functional requirement—enabling visibility and traceability of energy efficiency data—which is solution-neutral and within system control. Achievement of the target (E_target ≤ energy_per_ton) is a performance acceptance criterion, not a functional requirement; it depends on design choices (DP/IM) and operational context beyond the FR itself. This separation clarifies that the system must provide the measurement infrastructure; meeting the target is validated through acceptance testing.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verify that the NGHE solution collects energy consumption data (kWh or equivalent) and work output data (tons moved) during operation, computes energy_per_ton, and reports results in a format accessible to the Site Manager and System Engineer for comparison against the customer-defined E_target threshold over the agreed evaluation period.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall measure and log total energy consumed during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall measure and log total work output (mass moved) during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall compute energy_per_ton as the ratio of energy consumed to work output.
FR-NGHE-003.4: The NGHE solution shall report energy_per_ton and supporting metrics to authorized stakeholders (Site Manager, System Engineer) in a defined format and cadence.

candidate_dps: DP1: Instrument energy input (fuel/electricity) at source with calibrated measurement; instrument work output (tonnage moved) at point of delivery/completion; compute ratio offline or near-real-time via data aggregation pipeline; report against target threshold.

DP2: Deploy continuous energy metering on prime mover(s); deploy independent tonnage counters at load points; synchronize timestamps; calculate energy_per_ton via post-processing analytics; flag exceedances relative to E_target.

DP3: Embed energy and tonnage sensors within the equipment control system; fuse signals in real-time; compute and log energy_per_ton at each work cycle; provide dashboard and periodic summary reports.

DP4: Establish energy accounting via fuel consumption logs (manual or automated pump/tank monitoring) and work output via operator-recorded or automated tally; reconcile periodically; calculate energy_per_ton from aggregated period data.

DP5: Instrument energy at multiple subsystem levels (drivetrain, hydraulics, auxiliary); instrument tonnage at multiple load points; aggregate via hierarchical data model; compute energy_per_ton at system and subsystem granularity; enable root-cause analysis of inefficiency.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP2: Deploy continuous energy metering on prime mover(s); deploy independent tonnage counters at load points; synchronize timestamps; calculate energy_per_ton via post-process analytics; flag exceedances relative to E_target.

potential_sources: Timestamp synchronization between energy and tonnage subsystems—mitigated by using common time reference (GPS, NTP, or equipment clock) and loose coupling via data pipeline.

Energy metering placement: if metering is tightly coupled to prime mover control logic, changes to control strategy may require re-calibration. Mitigation: isolate metering as independent instrumentation layer.
Tonnage counter placement: if counter is embedded in load-bearing structure, mechanical changes may affect accuracy. Mitigation: use modular, replaceable counter design.

Analytics pipeline dependency: if energy_per_ton calculation is hardcoded in a single monolithic service, changes to reporting frequency or target logic require code changes. Mitigation: parameterize calculation and reporting logic.
coupling_check.notes: DP2 achieves functional independence by separating energy measurement, tonnage measurement, and analytics into distinct logical concerns. Timestamp synchronization is the primary coupling point, but it is manageable via standard time-sync protocols. The design avoids embedding measurement logic into control systems, reducing risk of unintended side effects.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Energy metering technology (fuel flow, electrical power, or equivalent) is available and can be integrated without disrupting prime mover operation. RISK: If metering introduces significant pressure drop or electrical load, it may affect equipment performance or require re-rating.

ASSUMPTION: Tonnage counters (load cells, flow meters, tally sensors) can be installed at load points with acceptable accuracy (±5% or better assumed). RISK: Harsh worksite conditions (mud, vibration, temperature extremes) may degrade sensor accuracy over time; requires periodic calibration and maintenance plan.

ASSUMPTION: Timestamp synchronization can be achieved to within ±1 second (or better, depending on work cycle duration). RISK: If work cycles are very short (<10 seconds), clock drift or synchronization latency may introduce unacceptable error in energy_per_ton ratio.

ASSUMPTION: Data pipeline (collection, storage, analytics) is available and can operate reliably in field conditions (intermittent connectivity, power constraints). RISK: Data loss, latency, or corruption may invalidate energy_per_ton calculations; requires robust error handling and audit trail.

ASSUMPTION: Customer-defined E_target is stable and measurable over the agreed evaluation period. RISK: If target is revised mid-evaluation or if evaluation period is too short to capture operational variability, acceptance criteria may be ambiguous.

ASSUMPTION: Energy and tonnage measurements are independent (no feedback loop between them). RISK: If operator behavior changes in response to real-time energy_per_ton feedback, measured efficiency may not reflect true equipment capability.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan and operational policies do not prohibit installation of metering or counting devices. RISK: If safety or operational constraints limit sensor placement, measurement accuracy or coverage may be compromised.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall enable continuous measurement of energy consumption (fuel or electrical) and work output (tonnage moved) with synchronized timestamps, compute energy_per_ton via post-processing analytics, and report whether measured energy_per_ton ≤ E_target over the agreed evaluation period, with documented calibration, data quality assurance, and audit trail to support customer acceptance. Acceptance criteria: (1) Energy metering accuracy ±3% or better; (2) Tonnage counter accuracy ±5% or better; (3) Timestamp synchronization ±1 second or better; (4) Data completeness ≥95% over evaluation period; (5) Calculated energy_per_ton satisfies ≤ E_target with documented confidence interval. This revision makes measurement independence, accuracy requirements, and data quality explicit, reducing ambiguity in DP selection and IM realization.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized energy metering, tonnage tracking, timestamp synchronization, analytics computation, and exceedance flagging for the entire system

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives continuous energy consumption data from prime mover(s) and tonnage counts from load points

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits computed energy_per_ton metrics and exceedance flags to monitoring/control systems

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Synchronizes timestamps across distributed metering points; may receive configuration updates for E_target thresholds

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emits alerts when energy exceedances pose operational or safety concerns

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. NGHE Solution is the system-level analytics and optimization platform. Energy metering, tonnage tracking, post-process analytics, and threshold comparison are core NGHE functions. Timestamp synchronization and exceedance flagging align with NGHE's role as the central intelligence layer. Minor diffusion: raw sensor data acquisition (hardware-level) resides in Vehicle; alert routing may involve SafetyServices.

Poor fit. Vehicle owns the prime mover and load-point hardware sensors, but does not own analytics, post-processing, or threshold logic. Placing DP here would scatter energy metering logic across vehicle firmware and require Vehicle to implement global synchronization and exceedance rules—violating separation of concerns. High diffusion: analytics, E_target management, and flagging would leak into NGHE Solution anyway.

Very poor fit. Teleoperation is a control/command interface, not an analytics platform. Placing energy metering and post-process analytics here would be architecturally incoherent. All core DP logic would diffuse into NGHE Solution and Vehicle.

Weak fit. Safety Services owns alert generation and safety rule enforcement, which aligns with the 'flag exceedances' aspect of DP. However, Safety Services does not own energy metering, tonnage tracking, or analytics computation. Placing the full DP here would require Safety Services to own data acquisition and post-processing logic, violating its focused responsibility. High diffusion: metering and analytics would remain in NGHE Solution.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The DP is fundamentally a system-level analytics and optimization function: it aggregates distributed sensor data (energy, tonnage), synchronizes timing, performs post-process computation, and compares results against a global target. This is the core responsibility of NGHE Solution as the central intelligence and optimization platform. Vehicle owns the sensors and actuators; NGHE Solution owns the logic that interprets and optimizes their operation. Containment is strong because all five sub-functions (metering deployment, tonnage counting, synchronization, analytics, flagging) are cohesive analytics tasks that belong in a single, system-level module.
containment_risks: Risk: Raw sensor data acquisition (hardware-level energy and tonnage sensors) is owned by Vehicle, not NGHE Solution. Mitigation: Define a clear Telemetry interface contract specifying data format, frequency, and timestamp precision. NGHE Solution consumes this interface; Vehicle implements it.

Risk: E_target threshold may be operationally dynamic (e.g., adjusted per mission or load class). Mitigation: Ensure NGHE Solution owns the E_target parameter store and exposes a configuration interface (via Network Link or Tasking) for authorized updates.

Risk: Exceedance alerts may trigger safety-critical actions (e.g., load shedding, emergency stop). Mitigation: Define a SafetyAlert interface contract. NGHE Solution emits alerts; Safety Services consumes and enforces safety rules. Avoid bidirectional coupling.

Risk: Timestamp synchronization across distributed metering points requires network coordination. Mitigation: Ensure Network Link interface supports time-sync primitives (e.g., NTP, PTP) and that NGHE Solution has authority to enforce synchronization policy.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP2 directly and completely addresses FR: it specifies the mechanism (continuous metering, independent counters, timestamp sync, post-process analytics, threshold comparison) to measure and report energy_per_ton against E_target. The DP is solution-neutral (does not prescribe implementation technology) and is verifiable against the acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target). No logical gaps detected.

diffusion_risks: DIFFUSION RISK (Minor): Raw sensor data acquisition (hardware-level energy and tonnage sensors) resides in Vehicle module, not NGHE Solution. Mitigation: Enforce strict Telemetry interface contract specifying data format, frequency, timestamp precision, and latency. NGHE Solution must not embed sensor-specific logic; it consumes only the contracted interface.

DIFFUSION RISK (Minor): E_target threshold management could leak into multiple modules if not centralized. Mitigation: Designate NGHE Solution as sole owner of E_target parameter store. Expose configuration interface (via Network Link or Tasking) for authorized updates; audit all writes.

DIFFUSION RISK (Minor): Exceedance alert routing may involve Safety Services for safety-critical actions (e.g., load shedding). Mitigation: Define SafetyAlert interface contract. NGHE Solution emits alerts with structured payload (energy_per_ton, E_target, margin); Safety Services consumes and enforces safety rules. Avoid bidirectional coupling or shared state.

DIFFUSION RISK (Minor): Timestamp synchronization across distributed metering points requires network coordination. If synchronization policy is not explicitly owned by NGHE Solution, clock skew and data ordering ambiguities may propagate. Mitigation: Ensure NGHE Solution owns synchronization policy and has authority to enforce time-sync primitives (NTP, PTP) via Network Link interface.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the correct IM owner (containment_score 0.92). All five sub-functions of DP2 (metering deployment coordination, tonnage tracking aggregation, timestamp synchronization, post-process analytics, exceedance flagging) are cohesive system-level analytics tasks that belong in a single, centralized module. Vehicle owns sensors and actuators; NGHE Solution owns the logic that interprets and optimizes their operation. The four minor diffusion risks are interface-boundary risks, not ownership risks—they are mitigated by explicit interface contracts and clear responsibility allocation, not by relocating the DP. No architectural incoherence detected.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: HIGH. The acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target over evaluation period) is directly measurable from NGHE Solution outputs. NGHE Solution owns the analytics computation and can emit timestamped energy_per_ton records and exceedance flags. Verification requires: (1) audit trail of energy and tonnage telemetry ingestion, (2) timestamp synchronization validation, (3) post-process analytics reproducibility, (4) E_target configuration audit. All are feasible within NGHE Solution's scope.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: CLEAR. FR allocation to IM is unambiguous: NGHE Solution is responsible for end-to-end energy_per_ton measurement and reporting. Vehicle provides sensor data via Telemetry interface; NGHE Solution consumes it and produces metrics. Safety Services consumes exceedance alerts and enforces safety rules. Worksite safety plan and operational policies are external constraints that inform E_target thresholds and alert routing rules, but do not alter the IM allocation. No competing ownership claims detected.

recommended_revisions: REVISION 1: Formalize Telemetry interface contract. Specify: (a) energy data format (units, precision, sampling rate), (b) tonnage data format and load-point identification, (c) timestamp format and precision (recommend ≥ 1 ms for energy_per_ton accuracy), (d) data freshness SLA (max latency from sensor to NGHE Solution), (e) error handling (missing data, sensor faults). This contract is the boundary between Vehicle (provider) and NGHE Solution (consumer).

REVISION 2: Formalize E_target parameter ownership and configuration interface. Specify: (a) E_target is owned and stored by NGHE Solution, (b) configuration updates are authorized via Network Link or Tasking interface with audit logging, (c) E_target changes are timestamped and versioned, (d) analytics retroactively tags energy_per_ton records with the E_target version in effect at measurement time. This prevents ambiguity in exceedance determination.

REVISION 3: Formalize SafetyAlert interface contract. Specify: (a) alert payload includes energy_per_ton, E_target, margin, timestamp, and severity level, (b) NGHE Solution emits alerts; Safety Services consumes and enforces safety rules (e.g., load shedding, operator notification), (c) no bidirectional coupling (Safety Services does not modify E_target or analytics logic), (d) alert routing and safety actions are owned by Safety Services, not NGHE Solution.

REVISION 4: Specify timestamp synchronization mechanism and authority. Recommend: (a) NGHE Solution owns synchronization policy (e.g., NTP server, PTP master), (b) Network Link interface supports time-sync primitives, (c) Vehicle and load-point sensors synchronize to NGHE Solution's time reference, (d) NGHE Solution logs synchronization status and clock skew metrics for audit and verification.

REVISION 5: Define post-process analytics algorithm and validation. Specify: (a) energy_per_ton calculation formula (e.g., sum of energy over evaluation period / sum of tonnage over evaluation period), (b) handling of partial data (e.g., missing tonnage or energy samples), (c) outlier detection and filtering rules (if any), (d) reproducibility: NGHE Solution must be able to re-compute energy_per_ton from archived telemetry data for audit and dispute resolution.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence, and the DP is logically sound and architecturally well-placed. However, the gate analysis has surfaced five critical interface-boundary risks and formalization gaps that must be resolved before proceeding to sub-level decomposition. Given the HIGH safety criticality of this function (energy efficiency monitoring directly informs load-shedding and safety decisions), these interface contracts and ownership clarifications are prerequisites to meaningful lower-level decomposition. Proceeding to sub-FRs without formalizing these boundaries risks propagating ambiguity and diffusion into child requirements. REVISE_CURRENT_LEVEL ensures that DP2 and its IM allocation are locked down with explicit, verifiable interface contracts before decomposing into sub-functions.

revise.why: DP2 is logically correct but insufficiently detailed for architectural realization. The five recommended revisions (Telemetry interface contract, E_target parameter ownership, SafetyAlert interface contract, timestamp synchronization mechanism, post-process analytics algorithm) must be formally incorporated into DP2 as explicit design specifications. These are not new FRs; they are clarifications of how DP2 is realized across module boundaries. Revising DP2 to include these interface specifications will eliminate ambiguity and enable clean sub-level decomposition.

assumptions: Vehicle module provides raw energy and tonnage telemetry via a contracted Telemetry interface; NGHE Solution does not embed sensor-specific acquisition logic.
E_target threshold is a configuration parameter owned and stored by NGHE Solution; updates are authorized and audited via Network Link or Tasking interface.
Timestamp synchronization is enforced by NGHE Solution via NTP/PTP or equivalent; all telemetry data is tagged with synchronized timestamps (≥1 ms precision).
Safety Services module consumes exceedance alerts from NGHE Solution and enforces safety rules (load shedding, operator notification); no bidirectional coupling or shared state.
Post-process analytics algorithm is deterministic and reproducible; NGHE Solution can re-compute energy_per_ton from archived telemetry for audit and dispute resolution.
Worksite safety plan and operational policies are external constraints that inform E_target thresholds and alert routing rules but do not alter IM allocation.
open_risks: INTERFACE RISK (Telemetry): If Vehicle module does not provide energy and tonnage data with sufficient precision, sampling rate, or timestamp accuracy, NGHE Solution cannot compute energy_per_ton reliably. Mitigation: Formalize Telemetry interface contract with explicit data format, precision, and SLA requirements.

INTERFACE RISK (E_target): If E_target parameter is not centrally owned by NGHE Solution, multiple modules may attempt to update it, causing inconsistency and audit failures. Mitigation: Designate NGHE Solution as sole owner; enforce configuration interface with audit logging and versioning.

INTERFACE RISK (SafetyAlert): If Safety Services module modifies analytics logic or E_target in response to alerts, bidirectional coupling will create maintenance and verification burden. Mitigation: Define SafetyAlert interface as unidirectional (NGHE Solution emits, Safety Services consumes); Safety Services owns safety rule enforcement, not analytics.

INTERFACE RISK (Timestamp Synchronization): If clock skew across distributed metering points exceeds 1 ms, energy_per_ton calculation may be ambiguous or incorrect. Mitigation: Ensure NGHE Solution owns synchronization policy and has authority to enforce time-sync primitives via Network Link.

INTERFACE RISK (Analytics Reproducibility): If post-process analytics algorithm is not formally specified or if partial data handling is ambiguous, energy_per_ton values may be disputed or non-reproducible. Mitigation: Formalize algorithm, outlier detection rules, and partial data handling; ensure NGHE Solution archives telemetry for audit.

SAFETY RISK (High Criticality): Energy efficiency monitoring directly informs load-shedding and safety decisions. If exceedance alerts are delayed, lost, or misrouted, safety-critical actions may be delayed or missed. Mitigation: Formalize SafetyAlert interface with guaranteed delivery, latency bounds, and structured payload; validate alert routing in safety case.
evidence_needed: Formalized Telemetry interface contract (data format, precision, sampling rate, timestamp format, freshness SLA, error handling).
Formalized E_target parameter ownership and configuration interface specification (authorization, audit logging, versioning, retroactive tagging of energy_per_ton records).
Formalized SafetyAlert interface contract (payload structure, delivery guarantees, latency bounds, routing rules, Safety Services responsibilities).
Timestamp synchronization mechanism specification (NTP/PTP or equivalent, time reference authority, synchronization status logging, clock skew metrics).
Post-process analytics algorithm specification (energy_per_ton calculation formula, partial data handling, outlier detection rules, reproducibility validation).
Safety case analysis confirming that exceedance alert latency and routing do not compromise safety-critical load-shedding or operator notification timelines.
Verification plan for energy_per_ton measurement accuracy, including audit trail validation, timestamp synchronization validation, and analytics reproducibility testing.
